speaker: interviewer
text: Interviewing Solomon Kupitz and this is tape number five, and I'm Bonnie Dwork.

speaker: interviewee
text: So as I was already past the police guards and I was already in the station, and we both go to the one that the girl who gave me in Kiev that led to her friend in Moscow. And this is on the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], that means that in the station which is completed.

speaker: interviewee
text: So yes, we came down. It was a beautiful railroad, beautiful station. The subway, I thought it a museum. And we were asking two Russian policewomen, where is the right Russian station, and she showed us straight to the train, and we came there.

speaker: interviewee
text: As we came to the address we-- I had, and we walked up a couple floors, there was no elevator. It's a 10 floor building without elevator. So we walk up, and-- and I knock the door, it was late in the afternoon. It was about 3 o'clock and nobody answered. So I was a little bit concerned, I hope I-- I don't have nowhere to stay or to be. And then we sit down on the steps and waiting until 8 o'clock in the evening and knock again. And here comes out this young girl, and she-- and I give her the letter. And she was very excited. And I told her who I am. She said, please come in. And she took us in the whole apartment, the whole-- it was only like a little room, like just a bed, a table, and a-- and a candle light on the table, and that's it. And she apologized that she is studying to be a doctor, she studies for the last weeks and weeks. She has nothing to eat much and she did not hear a knocking. So I said, don't worry. Whatever we had, we put everything on the table and they were very happy. And then, she was-- she had a mother, her mother was a doctor, and she came home later. And her father was [NON-ENGLISH] in the Red Army. And then I told her my purpose coming to Moscow. And when I told her, she got scared. She couldn't believe it. But she said that my mother will take you to the American embassy tomorrow and show you where it is, and you go in yourself. And I was very happy that she told me this, but then in the-- in the morning, we hear that there's gonna be a holiday that Eisenhower is coming in to Moscow to greet after the Second World War. And there was a special day to meet with Stalin, and we come close to the station, they tell us that it was all circled with the Russian police. We were not allowed to go into the center because I had to go to the American embassy. It is right near the Red Square. This was [INAUDIBLE] square of [INAUDIBLE]. So we went back the other day. She took me to the American embassy, and she-- we went out to the station. And she showed me where the American embassy is. And just you walk yourself.

speaker: interviewee
text: As I came close, I saw two policeman standing. I came over, and I said, I would like to go into the American embassy. And they just told me, go straight to the right, make a right, and there is the American embassy. I thought maybe they will arrest me, I thought what is a-- a Russian citizen doing there? But somehow God was with me and I got in.

speaker: interviewee
text: I came in, the door opened up, and a secretary came over, a young girl. And she said what I want. And I told her that I came to see the ambassador because my father is in the United States. And I have a visa to go to America. And I like to see the ambassador. And she told me that the ambassador was not in now, but she let me in. You wait here a couple hours until he'll come and he will see you. As I was sitting in the room where [? Awil Hare, ?] the ambassador who [? about to ?] come in. And I looked at pictures of President Roosevelt and everything and I said, if whatever is gonna happen, I'm not going to get out from here. Only death. I don't want to get out. I'm not-- I came for something, and I must see, in my life, my father. Here comes in the ambassador. And he comes in with the secretary who speaks Russian and English to translate. And then he's sitting across me, and he says, tell your story from the beginning to the end. How your father came to America, how old you were, and I told the whole story.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what language did you speak?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Russian. And he and she, they were almost crying. But on the end, he said he wishes he can help me, but he cannot because I am a Russian citizen. And to go out from the Soviet Union, I must have a Russian exit visa. And because I was born in Poland, I have to get-- I have to get a Polish passport and then the American embassy will give me the visa to go to the United States. But before all of those documents, if I don't have it, I should not come. I should not write. I should not call. Anything, because it will be trouble for the American embassy and for me because the Russians will watch me now, and that's it. So I-- I had to go out, I said goodbye.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I walked the corridor, the long corridor and think to myself, is this what I came to the American embassy? As I was walking and this woman, this secretary, her name was [? Barisvna ?], she run after me. She stopped me just before the door.

speaker: interviewee
text: She says, you are my child. I have a son now in the army. And you are now my child, and he is on the-- on the front in Japan. And I don't know what's gonna be with him. But you are mine, and I'm going to help you. Here's my address. Tomorrow night, I want you to come but nobody should see you. Watch when you go in before the door. And you'll come to my house. And I did not know what to do. I-- I kissed her. And I was crying, and I run out, and I run back to the house where I stayed with these people. And I came there, and I told them every-- they were waiting like to see me already, what's happening. And I told them. Tomorrow night, the mother again, took me to a different station. This was they called it, [INAUDIBLE], the node of Moscow. And I came to the station where she lived. The woman just showed me where to go. She left, it was at night. And I just knocked the door. She opened the door. She came to the door, and I looked at the beautiful home. And I see a table was made for me with food, white, everything beautiful. And I see her father, with white gray hair, and her mother sitting by the table. And she said, please, sit down, eat. And since at that time, I never saw food like this, I never what they prepared for me, but I couldn't eat. And I wanted to tell him the story.

speaker: interviewer
text: You couldn't eat because?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because of my anxiety of everything, to tell these wonderful people especially her, [? eventual. ?] And they want to know everything. And I told them from the beginning to the end. The father speaks many languages, her father. He speak French, Hebrew. They were from the old Russian aristocracy. And he said to me I can speak any language I want. Yiddish, Russian, Hebrew, French, English, anything. And I spoke in Russian. After I finish to tell the story, and I ate, she went in another room, [? Barisvna ?], Mrs. [? Barisvna ?]. She made a telephone call to, at that time, the Secretary of the Soviet Union, Molotov. She was on the telephone maybe 10 minutes. And she comes back, and she says-- she kisses me, and, you will see your father. But this time, you have to be here in Moscow maybe five or six weeks. All of these documents have to be made. The Polish embassy is not open yet. And the [? Wanda ?] [? Solesky ?], she was the president of the Polish government, which was the Communist government. As soon as the Polish embassy will open, I'll give you the address. I give you everything. She right away went to the typing machine, gave me letters. And by the way, you don't have no money, you have-- you don't have nowhere to be. I-- I give you a [? talone ?], a big paper, that you can come to the Hotel International-- international-- or eat there, or sleep there, or if you want to say with your friends, you can stay with them and take the food home and eat with them. And this is that big-- you rip off a little, they call it [? talone ?], a little-- like a--

speaker: interviewer
text: Ticket?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ticket, yes. And you'll be in contact with me as soon as you go to the Polish embassy. And then we will send you to the headquarters of the Russian KGB USSR, and the head of the KGB was Beria. You know the name of Beria. But don't come before-- before you have the Polish passport. But you have to go and see, go every day and check if the embassy's open. And I went to the Polish embassy every day, and I came to the Polish embassy. I saw many generals, Polish, which were Jewish, but dressed in Russian or Polish uniforms. The Polish embassy itself was not open yet, they told me to come back two weeks later. And this, I came back two weeks later, and there was the embassy was open. The head of the Polish embassy was Ambassador Ciechanowski. He was Jewish, from Krakow. And assistant ambassador, politically actually, was [? Yurena ?] [? Gafon ?], a woman, also Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the first I came in I have to see Mrs. [? Yurena ?] [? Gafon ?] to be interrogated. Why I want a Polish passport, and why do I want to go to the United States? So I came in the room. She told me, you are Russian now,. You can go.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, I was born in Poland. My land-- my homeland was Poland. That's where I came from. I was born there. Doesn't matter. You are Russian citizen, you don't want to go to a capitalistic, imperialistic country. You-- this is your future. You are young. You'll be educated. Here, we'll give you everything. I said, I want to see my father. I want to tell my father all this what I just told you. You can invite your father to come to the Soviet Union. I said, my father is old and sick. He knows already what everything happened. He cannot come. Well, we don't know if we're gonna give you a passport. And she was Jewish. I could not believe it. They were not friendly at all. And I left. I came back, and I made an appointment, I want to see the ambassador. OK. And I got the permission to see the ambassador, Ciechanowski. That was two weeks later. I couldn't get in touch with the secretary of the American embassy because I wanted to establish everything later to come. I came in, and they-- he invited me in his room. Opened the door, and I saw inside everything Polish. It remind me everything when I was a child. With the flags, and I couldn't believe that I am at the Polish embassy. And he says, tell me everything. Everything from the beginning to end, how your father went to the United States, and why you lived through the war. And I told him, and as I was telling him the story, maybe for four hours, he was crying. And you will see your father. But, you have to come back until everything-- you have to-- we just opened the embassy. And you have to have, we have to have all typed and everything. And then you'll have the passport, and you'll go to America. And I came back. They made for me a Polish passport. And I, with the passport, I went back to the American embassy. When I came to the American embassy, it was in the-- like in the afternoon. And I had the passport and I told-- and I saw [? Barisivna ?], secretary. First what she did, she called all the workers in the embassy, the secretaries, the assistants, to fingerprint me, to put on affidavits right the way that I have to go to the KGB. And also a letter to the American attache, military attache in Odessa. As soon as there's going to be an American ship to transfer me to the United States. And they made a party for me. After that all of this, [? Avril ?] [? Herimon ?] was there. He congratulated me what I did, that nobody could believe that a boy like you could do that. And I came into Moscow with not the right papers. And I'm going to the KGB headquarters in Lubyanka to see the assistant of Beria. The second day. And I come over there, big building, also not far from Red Square. I knew already where I'm running. Moscow was already like to me, like-- like I'm running around over here. Matter of fact, I was running so fast, when I came into the metro, I couldn't-- I was just jumping. And the doors opened up, and I jumped at that big general with his big medals. And I said, excuse me, I'm sorry. And he says, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. He said to me, please excuse me. I-- I almost-- I jumped in to him, I didn't see where I'm running. And he was so excited. I was excited. I see this general with all these medals right in front of me. And I'm coming to the KGB in Lubyanka. I come in the front door. Everything is gray. I see some people, young people waiting. And I'm going to the office in the corner, sitting there a woman. And she says to me, you have to give me all your documents, all your papers, that you carried. Nothing with you, empty. Don't carry one paper. Everything that you have, give it to us. Well, it's-- to me it's like when I was going to be shot. And I gave her everything. I gave her my passport. I gave her my permits which given out in Zhmerinka, which was not allowed to give. But this [? nachanik ?] KGB, because only the KGB, central Moscow, USSR were [INAUDIBLE]-- was allowed to invite you to Moscow, no other agency. And I'm giving her this. I'm giving her from the Polish patriotic organization. And I wait, and wait, and wait for hours. And I see, I'm sitting, some foreigners. Young girls, boys, Spanish, which was kept by the KGB. Of purposes that they want to go back to Spain. They came to Russia during the revolution in Spain as children. And they wanted to go back. They were not allowed. They told me don't say a word to nobody because everyone is a spy. Then they called me in. The doors open up, a gray room as big as this block. In the middle of the room is sitting a KGB lieutenant, a young guy in the 20s. With his stars, I knew right away. And I come to the table and he says, sit down. Tell me how you came to Moscow. He didn't want it directly. And he's holding the [? proposk, ?] the paper with the passport.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you came to Moscow? You, as a Russian citizen, did not know that to come to Moscow you have to have a permit from us, an invitation. Otherwise you are not allowed to be over here. So when he told me this, and do you know he says, I can send you right away to Siberia? Do you know what you did? So when he was talking to me like this I made believe I don't understand Russian too much, and I start to speak Polish. Because I speak Polish. And he says, why, you don't speak Russian God bless you. And I said, well, I-- I am Polish. I speak Polish better than Russian. But he says, you are so smart that this passport, you see, and the [? proposk ?], you knew how to get. But a [? proposk-- ?] a permit you did not know how to get? How did you get your passport? You are smart. You get out of this room within minutes. And within 24-- 21 hours, you have to leave Moscow. If you don't leave Moscow within 21 hours, you'll be arrested and sent away. Get out. So on the right side was a door, not that the door was open. And I run in the other room and this girl, his secretary was sitting there and she's holding her hands by the head. I can't believe it that he let you out. You are lucky. You are lucky. Don't worry. You have to go back to the Ukraine. So I said to her, how am I gonna leave? They got all my documents. How I'm gonna go back? And then comes after the rest-- the rest of the story. And she went to the typewriter. I said, I cannot back-- go back to Ukraine only if I have a permit from you. Otherwise, I will not get no ticket on the station because there's thousands of soldiers. How I'm gonna get into the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: She sat down. She wrote a letter. That letter was to the [? nachalnik, ?] to the chief of the station, to get me into the train as soon as possible to go back to Zhmerinka. And I said goodbye, but she said to me, you are a lucky man. I never saw over here in this room walks out-- out like you.

speaker: interviewee
text: She spoke very low, you are gonna go to America. I said, what about my documents? My passport? This will be sent back to you in Vinnitsa, to the headquarters of the KGB. Over there, you'll go to the KGB, and Kiev is capital of the Ukraine, is there own international foreign affairs. And from there you'll get the visa and go back as soon as possible.

speaker: interviewee
text: I run back, and I came into the house. The mother and the daughter could not believe. Same day I was running, they-- I said, somebody has to take me to the station, I don't know where. So the daughter took me to the station. This girl, supposed to be a doctor later on. I said goodbye to the mother. They were-- they were a mother to me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the girl wanted to go with me to the Ukraine for a vacation, it was in July. And I tried, but she could not. She could never go into the train. When I came to the station, there were thousands of soldiers. How am I gonna get with my ticket? So I run into the [? nachalnik, ?] chief of the station, and he orders a policeman to take me to the train because I already-- I had a document from the American embassy and from the KGB.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I came to the station. She's running with me, and he is going with his gun. And people, thousands, soldiers, civilians, they're pushing to the train. So he was going, get everybody on the side, American, American. He pushed me to a line maybe of five blocks of people, straight into the train. And she was crying. I wanted her to with me, to come to the Ukraine to be thankful to her. She couldn't get in, and she was just crying. And I got into the train. Going back, this was already a passenger plane--

speaker: interviewer
text: Train.

speaker: interviewee
text: A passenger-- a passenger train. I was so excited. I-- they gave me even food in the embassy. Tuna fish in-- in cans so I can have something to eat. And I was-- there was also in the train was already American diplomats, all kind. It took about two days-- two days. I came back to Zhmerinka. And I came back to Zhmerinka. The whole city was talking about me. Everybody. The KGB in Vinnitsa had received a warning and special documents, papers to arrest me and to fine me maybe about 5,000 rubles because I came illegal to Moscow. The KGB from Moscow telegrammed to the KGB in Vinnitsa not to give me anything and to arrest me. And to--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you know what the [INAUDIBLE] girl did with this papers? She received it, the one who gave me the letter to go to Moscow with her friend. She took this everything, and she ripped it and throw it into the garbage. Then-- this she told me later. Then I came to the KGB headquarters. What I'm do for that? I need my papers. And she told me in Moscow that I will receive in two weeks at the headquarters in Vinnitsa. So I was running after-- I-- I knew already the-- the-- the post office and the people who work there, so they knew me. I was coming every day asking, where is my papers?

speaker: interviewee
text: And one day they came in. So I run to the KGB, and the girl says she got all the papers. And don't worry. We got the passport too. But you have to see the chief, the head from the KGB over here. His name was [? Kwasnikov ?]. Meantime, I got two packages from the United States from my-- from my father, food, all kind of things, cans, I did not open up. I took this whole thing with me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I came in the headquarters in Vinnitsa to the [? NKV ?] there, KGB. And I come in, and I said I want to see [? nachalnik, ?] which is the chief of the [? NKV ?] there. And he calls me in. He's sitting with a table there. I sit on the other side. And first he says, what I'm carrying? And I put it under the table. He says, what is this? I say, this is from my father, and this is for you. He took it right away. Then he said, don't tell it to my assistant, the next KGB guy. That's how it was. What can I do for you? And I told him the story.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, don't worry, I'll get you the exit visa. We are in the Ukraine. Kiev is the headquarters. I called Kiev. They did not have a telephone right away, even the KGB that could be, they didn't have telephones like today. But they have to wait a day to get back the message. So he got in touch with Kiev, the headquarters of the KGB in Kiev, and they told him to make for me a special visa.

speaker: interviewee
text: Which, in Vinnitsa, the [? NKV ?] there did not have, no giving visa to no Russians since the revolution. So where they gonna get the stamp to put on my passport? They ordered a shoemaker to make for me a stamp. And I run for two weeks to see the stamp is dry. But how did they get the-- the letters? They found in [? the heives ?] from [? Nikolai ?], from [? Sir ?] [? Nikolai's ?] times, how a visa was to be made. And from that blueprint, they made the stamp.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I got the stamps two weeks later. I took it away from the shoemaker. I was looking only to get it dry. And I run back to the [? NKV. ?] And I told-- I tell general, he was a general. [? Kwasnikov, ?] I have the stamp, the printing. Terrific. Sit down. He calls Kiev. Kiev gives him the permission. And now that he got the stamps, he hasn't got the ink. When you put it in. You put the stamp on the ink to make, they call it [? bichat ?]. So he had ink in a bottle, you know, because you write, you write with a pen over there. And you put in.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he put the ink on that thing. But he didn't put it right because he forgot it's liquid. And he puts the stamp, he opens my passport, and he puts the [INAUDIBLE], and he puts in my passport, and the whole thing is black. You don't see no stamp. And he made it on three, four pages of my passport.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when I-- when I saw this, I said there's gonna be trouble. He apologized and he wrote that document that I have over here. Because of technical things, he spoiled. But it does not matter. I'm going to Odessa. He-- he gives the orders to transfer me on the train to Odessa on this and this date. And over there, I should show it to the KGB over there, and they will let me through. And that's how it was. Couple days later I said goodbye Zhmerinka. The whole city was near the railroad in the morning at 6 o'clock. People which were with me in the ghetto. Some of them are over here. Many, some of them, many of them, already came after. And I left Zhmerinka to go to America. That was in the morning. I left 6 o'clock, I came in at night to Odessa. In the embassy, in Moscow, the secretary told me I should put my passport and sew it up in my jacket in the back because in Odessa, there's-- you've gotta be very careful, a lot of crooks, hooligans. They can steal by you the passport. That's what I did. I'm going to Odessa. I can't believe it. And I come in at night, and I have to go. I had a letter from the American embassy to go to the Hotel [? Gosteinsta ?] that means Hotel [? Londonskia ?], London, which is on the end of the city near the Black Sea. I did not know so late at night at 10 o'clock to-- where to-- how to go, but they told me you gotta go up this street, it takes you straight to the end, maybe five miles or six miles. And I walked to the end. And I came to the hotel. And I came in the hotel. There was office in the front. I walk to this, was a woman. I gave her my passport. I told her who I am. And she called down the American military attache from the second floor. His name was [? Herscher ?]. We have over here a passenger that has to go to the United States and please come down.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he came down. I didn't understand any. She spoke to him in English. Big, tall military man. And I give him the passport first. And then he opened up the passport. He said, what happened? What is this? He spoke Russian, and I told him what happened. And I had-- so he said, don't worry, you might have problem, but I'm gonna try to put you on the ship. OK, try to look at me rather than around the room.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
